title: Spec Home Selling Strategies, Lot Selection, and Market Timing
summary: Participants shared challenges and strategies around selling stalled spec homes in slow markets, with discussion focused on pricing tactics, seasonal timing, and listing presentation. The group also explored how to identify and evaluate quality lots, with a newer builder asking how to break into spec building and an engineer participant raising questions about systematizing the lot evaluation process.

teachings.concept: Percentage of Homes Under Contract as Market Health Metric
teachings.explanation: The proportion of active listings that are under contract is a reliable indicator of market velocity. A healthy market sits above 25-30%; current markets are seeing 5-20%, signaling slow conditions where even well-priced homes may take months to sell.
teachings.quote: The tipping point seems to be about 25 to 30%. So, like, in 2012 through 22, I had not seen a number below 30%. And right now, I'm seeing numbers like 5, 10, 15, 20.

teachings.concept: Price Reduction Rules of Thumb Based on Showing Activity
teachings.explanation: The level of market response to a listing indicates how far off the price is. No offers despite showings suggests 5% overpricing; no showings at all suggests at least 10% overpricing, requiring a more aggressive reduction.
teachings.quote: The rule of thumb showings, but no offers is 5%. No showings is at least 10.

teachings.concept: Taking the Loss to Preserve Capital Velocity
teachings.explanation: Holding a stalled spec home hoping for the right price costs more in carrying costs and lost opportunity than taking a strategic loss and redeploying capital into the next project. Builders who sold quickly during the 2008 downturn outperformed those who waited years for their target price.
teachings.quote: The people that just sold it took the loss, you know, they turned and turned and they turned their money like 10 times to like, like they made a killing. So you're better off taking the small loss and just move on.

teachings.concept: Seasonal Selling Windows for Spec Homes
teachings.explanation: Most markets have defined windows of peak buyer activity. Missing these windows can cost builders significant money. Builders should plan project completion timelines backward from these windows to ensure they list during peak demand.
teachings.quote: We have like a three month window where it's 10% higher than the rest of the year. And if we miss that window, it's like, I mean, on a million, $2 million house, $3 million house, that's a lot of money.

teachings.concept: First Five Photos Rule for Listing Presentation
teachings.explanation: Buyers scroll through listings like social media. If the first five photos do not hook them, they move on. Listings must lead with the five strongest features of the home to drive showing traffic.
teachings.quote: Change your photos. The description doesn't matter. Buyers will look through to look at the first five photos. First five photos would get them sucked in. Just think Instagram.

teachings.concept: Long Skirt vs. Mini Skirt Listing Strategy
teachings.explanation: Providing too many photos and too much information in a listing can cause buyers to rule out a home before visiting. Showing just enough to intrigue—without revealing everything—drives in-person showings where buyers can fall in love with the property.
teachings.quote: I think if you give too many pictures and too much information, sometimes I think that the people will rule out the house before going to see it. So it's kind of like, um, I think long skirt instead of mini skirt. Just, just enough where they want to go see the house.

teachings.concept: A-Plus Everything Rule for Spec Builds in Soft Markets
teachings.explanation: In a slow market, only spec homes that score high across all key criteria—location, lot quality, design, and price—will sell. Any weakness in one area compounds risk significantly. Builders should pass on B-grade opportunities rather than trying to compensate with construction quality or landscaping.
teachings.quote: If you've got like two A's and, you know, a negative or a B or a C, then those, those are going to sit.

teachings.concept: 20-25% Land Cost Rule Relative to ARV
teachings.explanation: A standard rule of thumb for spec builders is to pay no more than 20-25% of the projected after-repair or sale value for the land. This protects margin as construction costs are layered in.
teachings.quote: 20% is kind of the rule of thumb, 20, 25, kind of the rule of thumb that you want to pay for dirt on the ARV. So, like, $5 million house, you'd want to be spending around $1.2 to $1.4.

teachings.explanation: When underwriting a spec project, builders should target a 30% profit margin to build in buffer for market softness, cost overruns, and carrying costs. The realistic outcome may land around 20%, but anchoring to 30% protects the deal.
teachings.quote: Janine, the number, if you're penciling the numbers out, you want to make 30% profit. You might end up with 20, but the number to shoot for is 30.

actionable_advice: Search your zip code and price range to calculate the percentage of homes under contract versus total active listings to gauge true market velocity before pricing decisions.
If you have no showing activity, reduce price by at least 10% in one move rather than incremental reductions of 5% or less that fail to shift buyer behavior.
Price to a psychological price band such as $795,000 rather than an arbitrary mid-number to maximize search filter visibility and buyer perception.
Pull a stalled listing off the MLS during the slow season and relist at the beginning of the peak selling window with fresh photos and potentially a new price.
Change listing photos regularly to reset buyer perception; ensure the top five photos highlight the five best features of the home to drive showing traffic.
Consider buying down the buyer's mortgage rate and advertising that rate prominently in listing photos to compete with production builders offering rate incentives.
Stage key rooms of a completed spec home rather than leaving it vacant to achieve higher price points and better photography.
For builders looking to break into spec without full capital risk, contact owners of vacant lots via handwritten letters and offer to partner or place a sign, capturing buyers who will want to build.

Drive or walk target neighborhoods and compile a list of distressed or vacant properties, then send multiple rounds of handwritten mail followed by a direct phone call using skip-tracing tools like TruePeopleSearch.com.

Visit a potential lot at sunrise, sunset, and during a heavy rainstorm to understand real neighborhood conditions including traffic, neighbor activity, drainage, and flooding before committing to purchase.

When evaluating lots, immediately identify the highest price a finished home could realistically sell for on that lot, then work backward to determine maximum allowable land cost and construction budget.
client_challenges: Tiffany's spec home in Washington, Utah has been on the market over 200 days with no private showings and minimal open house traffic, listed at $885,000 in a price band ($600K–$1M) that is not moving in the current St. George market.

Tiffany listed her home during construction and accrued over 200 days on market, which now signals to buyers that something is wrong with the property even though the actual issue was timing and market conditions.

Albert is completing a project in New Orleans that will be delivered in January, outside of his local peak selling window between Mardi Gras and Jazz Fest, and is evaluating whether to pre-list using renderings or wait until the home is finished.

Janine, primarily a remodeler in Austin, wants to break into spec building but faces high entry costs (minimum $2–3M builds without land in her target neighborhoods), a soft higher price point market, and uncertainty about how to evaluate lot quality without experience.

A roundtable participant noted that pre-listing a home during construction using renderings can generate interest but buyers in non-premium locations often lack vision and prefer to wait for a finished product.

Participants discussed the difficulty of systematizing lot evaluation criteria—most experienced builders rely on intuition combined with informal checklists, and newer builders have no structured framework to determine which lots are worth pursuing.

Patrick noted that in the past he took on B-grade lots thinking he could compensate with design or landscaping, and it consistently did not work out, but acknowledged this judgment came from 20 years of trial and error.
notable_quotes: On taking losses to stay liquid: 'The people that just sold it took the loss, they turned and turned and they turned their money like 10 times. So you're better off taking the small loss and just move on.'
On listing presentation: 'Just think Instagram. You flick through, you flick through. If you don't hook them on the first five photos, you're done.'
On showing just enough in a listing: 'I think long skirt instead of mini skirt. Just enough where they want to go see the house.'
On market protection: 'It's nice to be in a market that's protected. It doesn't have a lot of other spec homes. You want to drop the price before everybody else does.'
On profit targets: 'You want to make 30% profit. You might end up with 20, but the number to shoot for is 30.'

On lot selection instinct versus process: 'There's two parts. Part one is almost more of an art feel subjective—is it a nice street, intuition. But then I do have a checklist—is it in the floodplain, how wide is the lot, what does the soil test come in at.'
